name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah, while Allah is witness to what you do?’ (98)
```

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ تَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَآءُ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘O People of the Book! why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah, seeking to make it crooked, while you are witnesses [to its truthfulness]? And Allah is not oblivious of what you do.’ (99)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تُطِيعُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
O you who have faith, if you obey a part of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back, after your faith, into faithless ones. (100)
```

```arabic
وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُۥ ۗ وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ هُدِىَ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٠١)
```

```english
And how would you be faithless while the signs of Allah are recited to you and His Apostle is in your midst? Whoever takes recourse in Allah is certainly guided to a straight path. (101)
```

## (Commentary)

As you can see from the continuity of the context (سياق), the verses indicate that the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — either a group among them, namely the Jews, or a specific faction of the Jews — used to disbelieve in the signs of Allah and bar the believers from the way of Allah by presenting it to them as crooked (عوجاً) rather than straight, and by portraying the crooked, deviant path of misguidance as the way of Allah.

This was achieved by casting doubts (شبهات) upon the believers, through which they would perceive the truth as falsehood and the falsehood to which they were being invited as truth. The preceding verses indicate the matters in which they had deviated, such as their denial of the lawfulness of all food prior to the Torah, and their denial of the abrogation (نسخ) of facing Jerusalem as the direction of prayer (القبلة). Thus, these verses are complementary to the previous ones that addressed the lawfulness of food before the Torah and the fact that the Kaaba was the first house established for mankind. They contain a denunciation and a rebuke of the Jews for casting doubts and creating discord (تفتين) among the believers regarding their religion. They also serve as a warning to the believers against obeying them in what they invite them toward, lest they disbelieve in the religion, and an encouragement and exhortation for them to hold fast (يعتصموا) to Allah so that they may be guided to the path of faith and their guidance may endure.

It has been narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam, as recorded by al-Suyuti in *Lubab al-Nuqul* according to what has been said: "Shash ibn Qays — who was a Jew — passed by a group from the tribes of al-Aws and al-Khazraj who were conversing. He was enraged by the harmony (تآلف) he saw among them after their former enmity. He ordered a Jewish youth with him to sit among them and remind them of the Day of Bu'ath. The youth did so, and they began to dispute and boast until two men jumped up: Aws ibn Qarzi from al-Aws and Jabbar ibn Sakhr from al-Khazraj. They exchanged harsh words, both factions became angry, and they rushed to fight. This reached the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, who came and exhorted them and reconciled them. They listened and obeyed. Then Allah revealed concerning Aws and Jabbar: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن تُطِيعُوا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَانِكُمْ كَافِرِينَ** (O you who have faith! If you obey a part of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back, after your faith, into faithless ones. [3:100]), and concerning Shash ibn Qays: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ** (Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah...’ [3:99])."

This report is a summary extracted from what was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Zayd ibn Aslam in detail, and something similar has been narrated from Ibn Abbas and others.

In any case, the verses are more closely applicable to what we have mentioned than to the specific occasion mentioned in the narration, as is apparent. Furthermore, the verses mention disbelief (الكفر), faith (الإيمان), the testimony (شهادة) of the Jews, and the recitation of the signs of Allah to the believers, and the like; all of this is more suited to what we have stated. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **وَدَّ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ لَوْ يَرُدُّونَكُم مِّن بَعْدِ إِيمَانِكُمْ كُفَّارًا حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِندِ أَنفُسِهِم** (Many of the People of the Book desire to turn you back into faithless ones after your faith, out of envy from themselves... [2:109]). Thus, the truth is as we have stated: that these verses are a completion of the preceding ones.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ** (Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you deny the signs of Allah...’ [3:98]). What is meant by the signs (الآيات), by the evidence of the unity of the context (سياق), is the lawfulness of food before the revelation of the Torah, and the fact that the direction of prayer (القبلة) in Islam is the Kaaba.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ** (Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar the faithful from the way of Allah...’ [3:99]) until His saying: **عِوَجًا** (crooked). Barring (الصد) means turning away (الصرف). His saying: **تَبْغُونَهَا** means you seek the way. His saying: **عِوَجًا** (crookedness) refers to that which is bent or distorted (المحرف). The meaning is seeking the way of Allah as something crooked, devoid of straightness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَاءُ** (while you are witnesses). That is, you know that food was lawful before the revelation of the Torah and that one of the characteristics of prophethood is the changing of the direction of prayer (القبلة) to the Kaaba. In considering them witnesses (شهداء) in this verse, He has paralleled what was in the previous verse where He, the Exalted, considered Himself a witness over their actions and their disbelief. In this, there is a subtle point (لطف) that is not hidden: they are witnesses to the truth of what they deny, and Allah is a witness to their denial and disbelief. Since He attributed testimony to them in this verse, He replaced the conclusion of the previous verse—namely His saying: **وَاللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ** (and Allah is witness to what you do [3:98])—with His saying at the end of this verse: **وَمَا اللَّهُ بِغَافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ** (and Allah is not oblivious of what you do [3:99]). This conveys that they are witnesses to the truth, and Allah, the Glorified, is a witness over all.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith!) until His saying: **وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ** (and His Apostle is among you [3:101]). The "group" (الفريق) refers, as previously mentioned, to the Jews or a faction among them. His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ آيَاتُ اللَّهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُ** (while the signs of Allah are recited to you and His Apostle is among you) means that it is possible for you to hold fast to the truth that becomes manifest to you by listening to the signs of Allah and contemplating (التدبر) them, and then referring what remains obscure to you—due to a lack of contemplation—back to Him, or by referring initially to His Messenger who is among you, not veiled from you nor distant. It means seeking the manifestation of truth by referring to him and then nullifying the doubts cast by the Jews, and clinging to the signs of Allah and His Messenger. Holding fast to these two is holding fast (الاعتصام) to Allah; and whoever holds fast to Allah has indeed been guided to a straight path.

Thus, the meaning of disbelief in His saying: **وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ** (And how would you disbelieve...) is disbelief after faith. His saying: **وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ** (while [the signs of Allah] are recited to you) is an allusion (كناية) to the possibility of holding fast (الاعتصام) in order to avoid disbelieving in the signs of Allah and His Messenger. His saying: **وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِاللَّهِ** (And whoever holds fast to Allah...) serves as a universal major premise (الكبرى الكلية) for that. The meaning of guidance to a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — is the attainment of a firm faith, which is the path that neither differs nor fails in its purpose; it unites those who walk upon its level surface and does not allow them to exit the way and go astray.

The use of the past tense in His saying: **فَقَدْ هُدِيَ** (he has certainly been guided), while omitting the agent (الفاعل), indicates the certainty of the action's occurrence without focusing on the specific agent.

It becomes clear from the verse that the Book and the Sunnah (السنة) — *the recorded words, actions, and silent approvals of the Prophet* — are sufficient in indicating every truth in which one might potentially go astray.
